fact: In the Northern Hemisphere, this date falls just 2 days after the winter solstice, when Earth is still tilted at its most extreme angle away from the sun.
Polar bears in the Arctic are dealing with some of the longest nights of the year right now - up to 20 hours of darkness in some regions!
December 23 marks the time when many evergreen trees like pine and spruce are typically producing their stickiest, most fragrant sap to protect against freezing.
Why do sunsets look extra orange and red around this time? The sun sits lower in the sky, so its light travels through more atmosphere, filtering out blue light!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, December 23 brings some of the longest daylight hours of the year - perfect beach weather!
Many hibernating animals like groundhogs and bears are in their deepest sleep phase right now, with heart rates slowed to just 8 beats per minute.
December 23 falls during Capricorn season in astrology, represented by a goat that's supposedly great at climbing mountains and being stubborn.
Snowflakes forming around this date in cold regions are more likely to be the classic six-pointed star shape because of the specific temperature and humidity conditions.
In northern Alaska, the sun doesn't rise at all on December 23 - it's been dark for weeks and won't see sunlight until late January!
Many deciduous trees are at their 'most naked' point right now in northern climates, having dropped their leaves weeks ago and standing like bony skeletons against winter skies.
December 23 sits in the final week of autumn in the Southern Hemisphere, when many flowering plants are preparing for their big summer bloom.
